reviews.review_text:
"Not responding" Bought the game and all the dlc. On a brand new pc. It's entirely non-functional. having reinstalled, verified, relocated. Nothing works but i can play just about all new games in ultra. 

Edit: After i wrote a post on the discussion forum of my issue one of the devs near instantly got in touch with me with suggestions of how to get it running again, it ended up working and with how invested they seem in making sure people have a good experience with their game i gotta admit i can only endorse it.
This game is a great dungeon crawler and a great rougelike; however, its pretty boring without friends

People say how difficult the game is, but its difficulty does not come from the enemies. It comes from the tedium of staring at the wall while your mana/health regen takes 3-5 business days just to get half your mana/health bar back. You can fix this later on with potions, but you need to find an alembic/cauldron and have plenty of water which you will need to bottle which also means you need to find the tinker kit/station to make the bottles. Sure some classes start with one or the other but never both. I have tried to enjoy this game, but struggle to actually have any fun with it to the point where i can't even understand how someone could enjoy this game at all.
Ive had it for a long time now, but only recently reaquired the RPG itch, and its a roguelike? Say less.

this was my first game on pc. i lost a account but got this one not long ago. This is the best game i have. i love it. and i have never played another game more than this one. i have never won once lmao. i cant a stop rushing and it ends up killing me every single time. i have only had a couple thoughts on what i thought could be interesting to add  and every single time i find a possible issue, it is fixed shortly because someone else already noticed it. 

it would be interesting if when you are the skeleton, you are neutral or friendly to all other undead, and then be hated by everyone else. or if a new class coule be created regarding necromancy it would be awsome to revive the last killed enemy.

if you could be a slime, that would be very cool. i yearn to bounce around. disolving all items for food/mp or maybe possibly a new rescource?(biomass perhaps?) and split and have my "clones" be followers. hated by litterally every other race exept other slimes. (possibly bats? it would be cool to have some sort of interaction with them since no other race does). 

Thank you for this wonderfull experiance of a game. I hope this game continues to flourish and thrive!

Found it because of Reggie, have to say it's easily one of the best games I've played in recent times, there is a certain learning curve figuring out the PvE aspects, alongside the various skills. But! With each dungeon run you gain more knowledge which makes every run that much easier. I will say once you find a path of progression, you (mostly) follow that path, the different races do result in differing playstyles. Lots of love has obviously been poured into this game so shouts out to the dev team at Turning Wheel.

TLDR good game, bad inventory issues. Prepare to sit in menus for a long time throwing away everything even armor and weapons. There just isn't enough room and weight is an issue. This is damning in a game that is basically timed as well with hunger, minotaur etc. It might not be an issue in COOP i'd imagine, but it is a massive issue in single player. Even if you used npcs to try to carry what little they are able to, they usually die pretty often screwing you over.

Suggestion: Maybe reagent bags? There is a game called Pixel Dungeon which is very similar to this game that fixes this by adding different bags that hold specific things. Potion rack, scroll carrier ETC. This way, the stuff is organized, (saving you time seeing where things are) and the space is increased cause as it stands you essentially have about 8 slots you can use if you are very frugal and keep only the bare minimum.

This game is great with all the different classes and races, all the different ideas and skills. Love that the skills level up over and time. There is so much depth with the alchemy, tinkering (wow make your own turrets and traps? a mini robot copter that gives light and detects enemies? awesome) so many cool items... items... wait a second... this is inventory management THE GAME. I would recommend this game but I swear I spend most of my time in the inventory. On average, youll be carrying: food (usually doesnt stack), heal potion, cure ailment potion god help the poor people who use alchemy who have to carry water bottles and a potion mixing tool AND empty potion bottles, spell books (often that you have to save for later as your skill isnt high enough yet) only one ring slot so assorted rings, a digging tool, a lighting tool, a couple gems to sell (that take 10 minutes to identify) a ranged weapon or worse, a few different throwing weapons that dont stack together, (maybe bandaids but honestly its not worth the inventory slot) ammo, a tink kit if u find one ( a must), a 2nd weapon for different enemy resistances, shield, some lockpicks (which start to not stack as their durability changes) a few scrolls (remove curse, repair, ID, food summon for sure), and likely, (tin opener? nah not worth the space) a magic staff of some kind, so likely you will be forced to carry about 16 items at the bare minimum, which leaves you with 8 slots, hopefully you dont intend on carrying spare armor or weapons too as there is also a weight limit on top of all this. Did I mention there are like 5 different key types that you have to carry around that don't all stack together either?

Youll end up spending a minute looking through the inventory while the hunger is ticking down, the minotaur timer is ticking down, finally forced to throw out gear I could use in the future (cause the durability is ruthless) and then walk 10 steps to be forced to look again cause I found like 5 more items on the ground. You even have a skill that youll need to level that encourages you to pickup every single thing you see as each item gives xp, it's also on a timer so you may have to sit and wait to ID them before throwing them. Without it you can't identify anything and would have to rely on rare scrolls to take up more inventory. What really sucks is you may not even find a shop to sell some of the crap you find and even if you do a lot of shops will only buy like 10% of the items you have. What finally sold me is the realization that most of the stuff I am forced to throw out is gear like armor and weapons and spell books and such which in most games you should be excited to find and keep but they have no point if you're stuck throwing them out. I have heard from guides people like to walk around with no chest armor because its too heavy, that's just messed up that people have to resort to that cause there isn't enough weight to carry much. I looked to see if there was a mod to deal with this but there isn't. It just really sucks to go through, for example, a chest checkpoint or a loot room and end up having to leave everything behind walking through the level seeing your piles of loot on the ground cause you just can't afford to keep that amulet of water breathing that may come in handy some day. Would love to try the alchemy or the tinkering but it would be item hell throwing so much out. So many scrolls and potions with helpful effects like invis, flying, healing, that I just can't take cause I have to hold on to the green orb that I have to keep and can't remove until way later.

The durability makes the situation much worse too because you have to sacrifice what little room and weight you can carry to TRY to carry replacements. They do have repairing in the game but you need MAX (or just about max it) repair skill (Tinkering, which isn't that hard to level but it too encourages picking up as most stuff as possible, at least with it you can dismantle stuff for parts but youll have to be lucky enough to find a kit to even use tinkering in the first place as you may never find one for the whole game) for most items, like why? might as well not have it.  There is also a backpack, but it is tied to being very skilled in that same skill you may not have, nor may you have the rare crafting kit (same kit) to make it in the first place. (there are also scrolls of repair but they repair like 25% of the dura) 
Truly it is unfortunate as the game is good but you won't spend much time playing it just sitting in your inventory crying over throwing away everything. You have hunger too, so some is on food, I think it said even GOLD weighs you down. I am not sure how to fix this problem, but in all the games I have played this is the absolute worst inventory management I have ever seen. Maybe reagent bags? There is a game called Pixel Dungeon which is very similar to this game that fixes this by adding different bags that hold specific things. Potion rack, scroll carrier ETC.

Too bad that none of my irl friends are fond of this game due the unforgiving nature of roguelikes. Last time I tried this on split screen, one of them started whining after the game restarted the run by itself for the fifth time. As for I, I always love my hardcore dungeon crawlers with ruthless despair.

Would recommend if you want your friends to hate you for this. 9/10
Solid game. If you can clear the gnome mines you can beat the game, you get tons of exp & gold, skipping isn't worth it unless you're speed running.

boulder crunched my friend, I went to grab their stuff and got eaten by a slime, 2nd friend choked to death from putting on a necklace.
good game 10/10 would get crunched again
Fun game, but online has some serious connection issues.
Awesome game pretty big learning curb really wish they would add new game+ with randomized loot to make the game even more spicy

I bought Barony quite a long time ago.  At the time of writing this review, it's 2 weeks away from the 11th anniversary of its initial E,A,(?) release.  When I bought it at the time, it was a nifty, gimmicky little thing that you could play with your friends and laugh as you watch them get instakilled by boulders. Now, it's still that, but with a litany of improvements over the many years it's been out; it's far more depth-filled, and to the point, far longer. The start of this year shipped a free expansion to the game that it sorely needed, and is the reason for me finally reviewing it. I absolutely cannot wait for Part 2 of Instruments of Destruction; apparently, it'll be adding even more places to explore, gear to collect and use, as well as representation of the new paid DLC's playable races as NPCs. Currently, they're not in the game, so some playable races just outright can't recruit anyone other than automatons, so this is something I'm particularly excited for as someone who likes to build big parties of lackeys to tank all the hits for me.  

When I first bought this game, I played for a total of 11 hours or so and had my fill. Now I've got 71 hours total, with 50 of those being the last couple weeks. It's really addictive. It's really good as a classic dungeon crawler. It's janky, but it's consistent in what it expects from you and what you can expect from it. Your mastery of the game will show, and sometimes you'll just die because of bad luck regardless. That's okay. 

Barony is easily a top 5 roguelite for me, and I think it's in the #1 spot, solely because I don't have too many enjoyable roguelite experiences. It's very fun with friends and all the paid DLC - while a bit pricey - adds a lot to it.  Definitely recommend. The 4 pack is less than the price of a current triple A game, and if you want more, the paid DLCs will give it to you, and soon we'll get Part 2 of Instruments of Destruction. Give it a shot.
Posted over 500 hours gameplay between family members since buying at the end of last year and still going. Best old school co-op dungeon crawler bar none. Give us more!!!!

The gameplay is compelling, but hateful. I'd like to not recommend the game, but it's good for what it is; a stupidly punishing roguelike. If that's your thing you'll like it, I don't. It's definitely a 'Git Gud' kind of game, and not a 'have fun' one.
It's sort of fun with friends, which was the reason I got it, but the solo experience kind of sucks. I feel like with the DLC, and some mods it could be a much better time, but as for the intended experience it just feels like I'm wasting my time.

Barony's challenge, secrets, surprises and interactive systems make it everything you could want in a dungeon crawler. 
It's obstacles will require you to think and act strategically, manage resources, and consider how to use the dungeons vast amounts of treasure and trash to its fullest. When playing co-op, teamwork is optional for play, but essential for success; you and your other hapless, dungeon-fodder nincompoops will have to distribute loot to who can use it best, ration supplies and sometimes plan out encounters with tougher monsters or more devious dungeon gauntlets.
Though the core of its combat is simple, consisting of timing hits and blocks, use of terrain, traps, potions, magic and found hirelings adds depth and encourages having a (sort of) plan.
The class system mostly consists of pre-set ability scores, skill and equipment, and rewards players for playing to their classes strengths while letting them branch out if they find the right gear. 
Deaths can be tense, and occasionally disheartening, but its secrets and fun progression make starting another run more appetizing than frustrating. Some stories that can shake out of odd and encounters and mistakes can be great, like being stuck with a cursed ring, only to be relieved of it by having it stolen by a succubus. Even after ending a long run, the promise of delving further, discovering a secret hidden deep under Hamlet or generating another improbable doomed expedition story out of Barony's systems kept me coming back.

If you're looking for a dungeon crawler that tests grit and wit with a healthy dose of action, Barony is an excellent choice.

This is a game kinda reminiscent of D&D type games. So many fun races and classes to choose from, so many interesting mobs, bosses and maps. I love how the game feels as a whole. Definitely give this game a try, especially with friends.
I like the way this sucks. This a game you play with friends and have fun losing. I can't recommend it enough.

game has a surprising amount of depth and each layer and each run is distinct. my first note of advice: play the tutorial levels. second is to familiarize yourself with the range of your weapon as it is further than you may realize. one thing I find is critically frustrating is the appearance of gems--they take forever to identify, appear everywhere but you can almost never sell them to a trader without max trading. I would even accept a penalized way to sell them for 50% if we reached 50 trading or something less, and all traders should accept gems. Gems are more precious than gold and it doesn't make much sense that you can't sell them to every vendor given how utterly frequent you find them. So many special creatures drop gems and it might as well drop nothing because holding onto a gem for several levels in hopes that you can sell it later is a dead spot in your already limited space in your bags. Barring certain specific interactions, gems are a "rare valuable" piece of trash in the inventory. I get encouraging players to grind a skill for exp, but unless you are at the right vendor or have max trading or a specific interaction this is the least realistic aspect of the game. the DLC classes are very good and worth buying/playing to unlock for all races. Food/Hunger feels a little bit overbearing at times as you may get extremely unlucky and luckily this is a slider feature you can turn off, because that lets you explore at your own pace. I think campfires should be turned into cooking spots (because duh, it's a campfire) with reduced effect as an alchemy table(which has more specialized equipment) rather than campfires being a dump of several torches you can take. that would let you turn a bunch of food into Rations. If you have a campfire and a frying pan you should be able to cook regardless of torch count in your inventory. Most of these 'criticisms' are quality of life feedback and don't actually detract from how much fun this game is. I can spend hours doing runs of virtually any class/combo and feel like there is enough variety and progression that I'm never unsatisfied.

Really good friendslop, technically you can play alone but I wouldnt recomend it. Theres plenty of different ways to play and types of classes that will give you allot of replayability, the levels are procedurally generated layout wise but it gives you set level types. While i hate generated levels its acceptable knowing what kinds of enemies and difficulty to expect, and theres obviously rng when it comes to loot and treasure I think they also did that correctly where it seems much more mercifull than other games so your not completely at its mercy. There are also about 35 floors so its like 5-7 hours to beat the game with 13 free classes and multiple ways to play some classes so you get allot of playtime. If its on sale with some the dlc you can get a good 60+ hours of of the game using just five characters since your gonna loose quite a few times (in a funny way)

I waited way too long to play this!!

Phenomenal adaptation of the roguelike genre to be easy to get into and play without sacrificing any of the challenge. I hesitated on playing it for so long because I was worried about the difficulty and knowledge requirements, but discovering everything has been a blast and the difficulty hasn't turned me off at all (there are handy run options besides.)
DLC is just additional races and classes, not necessary to enjoy, but very enjoyable and worth it nonetheless. 
Runs excellently on Steam Deck.
The learning curve is very steep but once you get a hang on the controls and different aspects its a very fun and entertaining game with a variety of play styles with or without friends

I'm writing this after about 130 hrs. i have managed to beat the game a single time and gotten past the hamlet a hand full of times. Ill be blunt, In all honesty the game can be extremely hard and unforgiving, But overcoming those odds can feel amazing. With the long time invested with each run it can feel devastating when you die to a boulder trap because you couldn't see or a mob triggered it causing it to roll across the map and you just happened to be in the one spot it would get you. But this is offset by the fact the only thing that really transfers between runs is your achievements and just the rank on the leader board. There isn't a resource that gets accumulated between runs that powers you up but a compendium that just gives you more info on mobs/traps/floors/items/magic just about anything. This game DRASTICALLY rewards game knowledge. Each floor is "random' but on certain floors there will be predetermined tiles/landmarks that are generated. It definitely has a steep learning curve especially if your all about going fast to get through the game, (speed runners aside) but if you slow down and take each fight like your first it helps tremendously. If your only into fast paced games this isn't going to be the one for you. But if you like to be methodical and plan a course of action you will have a grand time. The game honestly reminds me of a book series "Dungeon Crawler Carl" just less politics and foot stuff.

TLDR:
This game will batter you about like your step dad when you tell him no after he asked you to get his happy juice. But dear god does that man know how to cook! Some of the best food you've had in a long time!
this game is wild, if you are playing with friends, TURN ON PROXIMITY CHAT. its so funny listening to your friend go AHHhhh.... as they run from a boulder. great game

I love this game. It's like if Minecraft and Binding of Isaac had a 3d baby.

Although I warn you, you will never 100% this game and you will also get all of the DLCs

This game was clearly a work of passion and thoughtfulness. I play TTRPG's like D&D and also enjoy video game RPG's like Skyrim. This game is a beauteous mixing of simple dungeon crawl ethos and mechanics to capture an immersive and challenging experience. I could not recommend it more if you enjoy what it's trying to deliver.

I've seen some comments bashing the game for being "inventory management - the game" and other niceties. If a grindy-crawly game based around numerous kinds of difficulties beyond just combat - like hunger management, skill progression, and environmental obstacles - then I could definitely see one not enjoying the game. 

However, I would not call the game bad or not recommend it purely because not everyone will like what it's seeking to accomplish. What Barony does, it does well. It forces you to make decisions as though you truly are in a dungeon away from society and its comforts. It does not require deep engagement to play but rewards curiosity and experimentation. It provides guidance tastefully but makes you learn by doing to truly "get" what's happening. 

If the gaming industry followed the concepts, mechanics, and design philosophy of Barony today, we would all be better for it. My wife and I have sunk several weekends into this game and we always say it's a shame that there are only a thousand or so online players at any given time we play. I hope this review can contribute to the game's popularity and bring others the joy we've been given so far. Thank you designers of our new favorite dungeon crawler!

Went into a run where I died to a boulder, went into another, immediate boulder trap death. Went into a third, another boulder rolled unto me outta seemingly nowhere. On my fourth run, I actually made it past the first floor of the dungeon, then died to another boulder triggered by a rat. I quit the game and had to walk away to breathe and grab a drink because I was so absolutely furious. It was so ridiculously unfair and stupid that I was speechless for a while.
Then came back and booted it up again. All in all 10/10 game. I hope you get many boulders in your future as well.

you cannot intuit your way out of danger, you must let the devs troll you over and over, stealing your runs for the mega lolz. i can just see them now, rofling and lmaoing, twiddling their moustaches as they engineer some of these nonsense scenarios. "hmm that's a nice run you have. would be a shame if something...hmm... happened to it." they have way too much fun trying to kill you. i wanna be the guy levels of trial and error ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. i'm not even talking about cursed amulets of strangulation or mimics, you'll walk into an empty, indistinct room and boom, metal bars lock you inside and 3 walls explode to reveal boulder traps that kill you instantly. or perhaps you feel it's okay to pick up that pair of boots? well well well *adjusts fedora*, you should have logically guessed that right clicking those boots would trigger a horde of ghouls with vampiric hunger to spawn and flood the room. nothing personal, kid. seriously, how do you guard against these fixed instances as a new player? 

one of the most fun aspects of the game is that it gives you the power to break the rules. if you had the foresight to haul a pick axe with you all the way to this locked room, congratulations, you can break the wall and forget about needing to find the key. or maybe you were lucky enough to find a ring of levitation to float across a chasm to access a chest. but then there are these absolutely baffling moments where the developers suddenly decide to U-turn on this philosophy and you'll get an actual message at the bottom of your screen equivalent to "sorry, you entered a no fun allowed zone. please complete this level exactly as we intended". moments that just feel totally counter to the spirit of the game.

resident evil 1 tier voice acting and dialogue that gets worse the further you get. 

softlocks. 

my little pony referenced not once, not twice but three times... ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ jesus christ i'm so tired. 

annoying achievements like "beat the game without using the most fun aspect of X character race". "get killed lol". "wilfully compromise your run in the following idiotic way". "buy all of our DLC". 

speaking of which, you buy their DLC only to find out that you have to beat the game (this can take several hours) with that specific race in order to earn the freedom to mix and match and come up with your own fun race/class combos. like paywall me or put unlockable content in the game but for the love of god, [b]don't combine the two[/b]. i paid money for the extra ♥♥♥♥, let me use it however i like. 

but if you're willing to look past all that and accept that it doesn't give a damn about your time investment or the concept of fairness or your ideas about good game philosophy... it's pretty awesome

The dying is a learning experience though it can feel like a boulder death sim when first starting out fs

This is the first roguelike that I have been able to play for months and months without beating or actually making good progress. It is a learning experience as you die over and over in the dungeon. And this is fun. The magic is cool. The gear is all awesome and creative. The monsters are a bit repetitive but they are always scary. The game has secret levels. I like it a lot.

Recommended. Without hesitation.

Barony is one of those rare games that doesn't hold your hand, doesn't explain itself, and absolutely does not care that you just died on floor 3 to a cursed item you shouldn't have equipped. And somehow, that's the whole appeal.

It's a first-person roguelike dungeon crawler that feels like it was pulled out of the 90s in the best possible way. Procedurally generated floors, permadeath, dozens of classes, co-op for up to four players, and more ways to die horribly than you can count. Every run teaches you something. Every death is your fault. And you will never stop coming back.

The co-op is where it really shines. Dragging friends into the dungeon and watching them immediately open a sarcophagus or eat an unidentified mushroom is one of the purest joys in gaming. The chaos is part of the fun, and the game rewards players who actually work together and think before touching things.

Solo it's a tense, rewarding crawl through increasingly brutal floors. With friends it becomes something else entirely, part dungeon crawler, part comedy of errors, part genuine survival experience.

For the price this is an absurd amount of game. The developers clearly love it, the community is great, and the depth here will surprise you.

Just don't open the sarcophagus. You know what, actually, open it. You'll learn.

It'd be a bit of an understatement to say roguelikes are a crowded genre, but Barony is very much worth your time if you're looking for a game that hearkens back to the category's progenitor titles. There is no meta progression, intricate character building, or gameplay hybridization; it is a straightforward dungeon crawler where your objective is to reach the final floor without getting murdered by the creatures that occupy the place. In the vein of Rogue or Nethack before it, your primary advantage comes from learning how the dungeon's various systems interact with each other and exploiting every quirk you can. A lot of prior knowledge can be carried over from such titles, like never putting on an unidentified item, or knowing how to manage mana and hunger, or throwing potions that would otherwise harm you at an enemy instead of treating them as ignorable junk items. It lends itself to generally more patient and considered gameplay that may turn off those looking for something with more moment to moment punch. It's also good fun in multiplayer where the specialties of each class tend to shine much more compared to the often generalist approach that solo players will need to adopt. The DLCs provide interesting gameplay twists in the form of alternate classes and playable races other than humans, but aren't a necessary buy  if you're just looking to try to get into the game for the first time.
Very fun coop roguelite dungeon crawler. swim in the water trust
This game does not hold your hand or take it easy on you in the slightest. However once the flow of gameplay is understood you can get some very awesome moments of triumph.
This game will punch you in the nuts 15 times and leave you asking for more. Punishing and difficult as hell, but it is really fun.
